IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a meaningful problem—capturing character-level compositionality for logographic scripts—using a simple, broadly applicable visual-CNN approach and shows complementary gains to lookup embeddings on low-frequency characters. However, from a theoretical-purist perspective the work lacks formal justification for why rendered-image CNN embeddings should systematically capture compositional semantics, and key controls/ablations (CNN architecture, resolution, fusion alternatives) and reproducibility (code/models not mentioned) are incompletely reported, limiting confidence and uptake. The demonstrated improvements and cross-script transfer suggest useful practical value and potential follow-ups, but without deeper theoretical insight or rigorous controlled analyses the contribution is moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): This paper tackles a practically important problem—capturing character-level compositionality for CJK scripts—by proposing a general, easy-to-integrate visual-CNN character embedding that shows complementary gains to standard lookup embeddings and improves handling of rare characters across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. The approach’s simplicity and cross-script applicability make it useful to practitioners, but its reliance on substantial training data, limited low-resource benefits, and reduced transfer performance between traditional and simplified Chinese constrain its broader transformative potential. Overall, the work is a solid, practically-minded contribution that is likely to be built upon but not fundamentally change the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful problem—capturing character-level compositionality for logographic scripts—using a simple, broadly applicable visual-CNN approach and shows complementary gains to lookup embeddings on low-frequency characters. However, from a theoretical-purist perspective the work lacks formal justification for why rendered-image CNN embeddings should systematically capture compositional semantics, and key controls/ablations (CNN architecture, resolution, fusion alternatives) and reproducibility (code/models not mentioned) are incompletely reported, limiting confidence and uptake. The demonstrated improvements and cross-script transfer suggest useful practical value and potential follow-ups, but without deeper theoretical insight or rigorous controlled analyses the contribution is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a practically important problem—capturing character-level compositionality for CJK scripts—by proposing a general, easy-to-integrate visual-CNN character embedding that shows complementary gains to standard lookup embeddings and improves handling of rare characters across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. The approach’s simplicity and cross-script applicability make it useful to practitioners, but its reliance on substantial training data, limited low-resource benefits, and reduced transfer performance between traditional and simplified Chinese constrain its broader transformative potential. Overall, the work is a solid, practically-minded contribution that is likely to be built upon but not fundamentally change the field.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation to model description, experiments, and conclusions; main contributions are stated early and the methodology (36×36 rendering, CNN → GRU → softmax, fusion variants) is explained. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are informative for understanding results across zh_trad/zh_simp/ja/ko. I deduct one point because the paper lacks any formal theoretical justification for why visual embeddings should capture character-level compositionality — this absence of formal motivation is a presentation gap given the paper’s theoretical claim — and some claims about transfer/low-resource behavior could be clearer with more explicit illustrations or ablation summaries. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through method (rendering 36x36 character images → CNN → GRU → classifier) to experiments on Chinese/Japanese/Korean Wikipedia categories, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. Writing quality is generally good with no major ambiguity, though some discussion of limitations (e.g., training-data dependence and reduced traditional↔simplified transfer) could be made more prominent to aid reproducibility and practical deployment decisions. Overall clear and readable with only minor presentation improvements possible.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation to model description, experiments, and conclusions; main contributions are stated early and the methodology (36×36 rendering, CNN → GRU → softmax, fusion variants) is explained. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are informative for understanding results across zh_trad/zh_simp/ja/ko. I deduct one point because the paper lacks any formal theoretical justification for why visual embeddings should capture character-level compositionality — this absence of formal motivation is a presentation gap given the paper’s theoretical claim — and some claims about transfer/low-resource behavior could be clearer with more explicit illustrations or ablation summaries.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through method (rendering 36x36 character images → CNN → GRU → classifier) to experiments on Chinese/Japanese/Korean Wikipedia categories, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. Writing quality is generally good with no major ambiguity, though some discussion of limitations (e.g., training-data dependence and reduced traditional↔simplified transfer) could be made more prominent to aid reproducibility and practical deployment decisions. Overall clear and readable with only minor presentation improvements possible.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental results support the main claim that visual CNN-based character embeddings help rare characters and complement lookup embeddings, but they also agree important methodological gaps remain (lack of controlled ablations, stronger baselines, statistical significance testing, and limited low-resource/transfer evidence). Given these consistent shortcomings that limit the conclusiveness and generality of the claims, a modest deduction is warranted, so I rate soundness/correctness as 3/5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides experimental evidence that visually-derived character embeddings can complement lookup embeddings and help on rare characters across several CJK datasets, but it lacks any formal theoretical justification for why rendering characters as images should capture compositionality. The methodology includes reasonable baselines (Lookup) and fusion variants, yet the summary indicates missing controlled ablations (e.g., isolating CNN architecture, image resolution, or fusion design) and no mention of statistical significance testing or code release for reproducibility. Given those gaps and the authors' own reported limitations (reduced transfer and limited low-resource gains), the claims are partially supported but not fully validated by the rigor I expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported by experiments showing visual CNN-based character embeddings help rare-character instances and are complementary to lookup embeddings (with fusion improvements) across zh_trad/zh_simp/ja/ko. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check (so Q2 is moot), and the experimental methodology is reasonable (lookup baseline, ablations, multiple scripts) though I note missing stronger baselines (e.g., subcharacter/radical-aware embeddings), limited evidence for low-resource gains, and no mention of statistical significance testing. Overall the results justify the conclusions but the reduced transfer performance and some gaps in controlled comparisons warrant a minor deduction.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes fair, internally consistent comparisons against a standard character lookup baseline and evaluates across relevant multilingual datasets with sensible analyses (fusion, low-frequency/transfer). However, they also concur that important competing baselines (e.g., radical/sub-character, byte/Unicode or other structural models) and finer-grained ablations are missing, limiting claims about relative merit. Given the adequate but incomplete empirical comparisons, a middle score reflects solid but not fully convincing comparative evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its Visual CNN character embeddings against a straightforward character Lookup baseline and evaluates on the same datasets and accuracy metric, so comparisons are internally fair. However, it omits important competing baselines (e.g., sub-character/radical-based embeddings, byte/Unicode decomposition methods, or pretrained multilingual character models) and lacks stronger ablation controls isolating CNN/design choices; this limits the claims about relative merit. The authors do discuss related work adequately and analyze fusion and low-frequency failure modes, but the absence of these key baselines and finer-grained ablations prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its visual CNN character embeddings against a standard character lookup baseline on the same Wikipedia-derived multilingual datasets and reports classification accuracy, including fusion variants and transfer/low-resource analyses, so comparisons are fair and conditions matched. However, it only compares to a vanilla lookup model rather than stronger or more recent alternatives (e.g., radical/sub-character embeddings, byte/phonetic or other structural baselines) that are highly relevant for CJK languages, so important baselines are missing. The authors do acknowledge limitations and when their method underperforms (e.g., reduced transfer and low-data settings), and related work coverage is reported as adequate, but the empirical study would be more convincing with a couple additional, stronger baselines.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant problem and presents a practical method with useful multilingual evaluations, making it a good fit for an NLP/ML venue. I give a 4 because the applied contributions and empirical breadth are valuable, but the concerns about missing theoretical motivation, incomplete ablations/statistical analyses, and unclear reproducibility temper its suitability for top-tier acceptance as-is.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—learning character-level visual embeddings for logographic and syllabic scripts—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and targets the right audience (character representation learning for multilingual text). However, as a theory-focused reviewer I note the paper lacks formal/theoretical motivation or propositions explaining why visual CNN embeddings should capture compositionality, and key ablations/statistical tests (CNN architecture, image resolution, fusion choices) are not fully isolated. Reproducibility is also unclear (code/models not mentioned), which weakens fit for a top-tier venue that expects rigorous experimental controls. Overall the work is relevant but borderline due to missing theoretical analysis and stricter empirical rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: This is a strong applied paper that fits a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a practical, broadly applicable method (rendered-character CNN embeddings) for multilingual CJK scripts, evaluates on multiple languages (zh_trad, zh_simp, ja, ko) and tasks (Wikipedia topic classification), and compares sensible baselines and fusion strategies. From my practical multilingual-engineer perspective the emphasis on rare-character handling and complementary fusion is directly relevant, though reported limitations (limited gains in truly low-resource settings and reduced cross-script transfer) suggest the empirical story is not uniformly conclusive — hence a good-but-not-perfect fit.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the idea is an intuitive, practical application of existing CNN+RNN techniques to glyph images and not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The paper provides useful empirical evidence (especially for rare CJK characters and cross-script transfer) but lacks deep theoretical justification and extensive ablations or reproducibility guarantees. Given the moderate empirical contribution and primarily incremental novelty, a score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The core idea—rendering characters as 36x36 images and using a CNN to produce character embeddings fed into an RNN—is an intuitive application of standard CNN+RNN machinery rather than a fundamentally new method; fusion with lookup embeddings is also conventional. The paper lacks theoretical justification or formal propositions explaining why visual features should capture compositionality, and it appears to provide limited controlled ablations (CNN architecture, resolution, fusion choices) and no reproducibility guarantees, which together make the contribution mostly a combination of known techniques with limited novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The core idea—rendering characters as images and learning CNN-based character embeddings—is a concrete and practical application of multimodal grounding to character-level NLP, but it is largely an intuitive extension of existing character/glyph-aware embedding notions rather than a radical new paradigm. The paper does differentiate its contribution empirically (showing improvements on rare CJK characters, complementary gains via fusion, and cross-script transfer tests across traditional/simplified Chinese, Japanese, and Korean) which gives it moderate novelty. However, the method is straightforward (CNN over glyph pixels + GRU encoder + fusion) and doesn’t introduce fundamentally new modeling machinery, so I rate it as an incremental but useful contribution.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is solid—cross-lingual experiments, fusion comparisons, and sensitivity checks convincingly show the utility of visual character embeddings—so the paper makes a substantive empirical contribution. However, they also concur that theoretical justification is lacking, key ablations and statistical analyses are limited, and reproducibility is only partial (no code release), which prevents a stronger rating. Balancing these points yields a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to demonstrate the main empirical claims (multiple languages, fusion methods, training-size probes) but lack the theoretical analysis I expect to explain why visual CNN embeddings should capture compositionality, so I subtract points for missing formal justification. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are limited: key controls (CNN architecture, image resolution, fusion design choices) and statistical significance testing are not fully isolated, so experimental attribution is weak. Related work is reported as adequate, and the paper candidly discusses failure cases and limitations (transfer drop, data requirements). Reproducibility is only partial: hyperparameters are given and characters are rendered as 36×36 images, but code/models/rendering/config release is not mentioned, making replication difficult.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs solid cross-lingual experiments (zh_trad/zh_simp/ja/ko), compares visual CNN-based character embeddings to a strong lookup baseline, and evaluates fusion strategies—providing concrete evidence that visual embeddings help rare-character cases and are complementary. They include sensitivity checks (training-size experiments, script-transfer tests) and report notable failure modes (reduced transfer accuracy between scripts, limited gains in very low-resource settings). Related work is said to be adequately covered and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned and some important ablations (e.g., CNN architecture variations, more detailed per-frequency analyses or broader low-resource setups) are missing, so the empirical evaluation is strong but not exhaustively reproducible or explored.

main_claims: A character embedding model based on visual features (rendering characters as images and passing through a CNN) can capture character-level compositionality.
The visual-character embeddings improve processing of instances with rare/low-frequency characters in languages such as Chinese, Japanese, and Korean.
Visual embeddings are complementary to standard character lookup embeddings; combining them via fusion methods produces consistent improvements over the lookup-only baseline.
The method is generalizable to any language for which characters can be rendered as images and can transfer to related scripts (e.g., traditional↔simplified Chinese) though with reduced accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Each character is rendered as a 36x36 image, passed through a convolutional neural network to produce a visual character embedding, which is fed into a GRU-based RNN encoder and a softmax classifier; this Visual model is compared to a baseline character Lookup embedding model and combined via early/late/fallback fusion.
datasets: Wikipedia-derived dataset with 12 main categories (Geography, Sports, Arts, Military, Economics, Transportation, Health Science, Education, Food Culture, Religion and Belief, Agriculture, Electronics) for Chinese (traditional and simplified), Japanese, and Korean
limitations: Visual model requires sufficient training data for the CNN to learn useful visual features; with reduced training sizes the expected gains on low-frequency characters did not materialize.
When trained on traditional Chinese and tested on simplified Chinese accuracy dropped (~40% vs ~55%), indicating reduced transfer performance between scripts.
The paper reports that intersection of model performance across training sizes disagreed with their low-resource hypothesis, suggesting limited benefit in low-resource scenarios.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - character-level embeddings and text classification

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in theoretical machine learning and formal language processing with extensive experience proving properties of representation learning models.
traits: Penalizes papers that lack formal or theoretical analysis: will deduct points if there is no theoretical justification or formal proposition explaining why the proposed representation should capture compositionality (e.g., expected deduct of 1-2 score points).

Requires rigorous ablation and controlled experiments: will penalize (subtract points) if key components (CNN architecture, image resolution, fusion methods) are not isolated with full ablations and if no statistical significance tests are reported.
Skeptical of primarily engineering/empirical novelty: will rate lower if contribution is an application of known methods (CNN+RNN) without new algorithms or theoretical insight.
Strict reproducibility standards: will lower score if code, trained models, or exact rendering/configuration details are not released or reproducible (expects concrete release plan).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize theoretical motivation, formal insights, and tight experimental controls; empirical gains alone must be explained by theory or novel methodology to get a strong score.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Multilingual Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher focusing on multilingual and low-resource systems, with experience building production models for languages with large character inventories (CJK) and working on multimodal embeddings.
traits: Rewards strong empirical improvements on low-frequency/rare-character cases and multilingual benchmarks: will give bonus points if the method demonstrably helps rare/occasional-character instances across multiple languages.

Values simple, general, and practical methods: tends to score higher when an approach is broadly applicable (works for any renderable script) and easy to integrate (expects CNN+RNN simplicity as a plus).

Favors novel use of complementary modalities (visualization of characters) and empirical fusion strategies: will rate higher if experiments show the visual embeddings are complementary to standard embeddings with consistent gains across datasets.
Forgiving of limited theoretical analysis if experiments are thorough and include realistic baselines, transfer experiments (e.g., traditional↔simplified), and ablations demonstrating robustness.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize practical impact, empirical robustness across languages and low-resource regimes, and methodological simplicity that enables real-world application; strong empirical evidence can outweigh lack of formal analysis.
